graph_label: EA-OS (consulting agency operating system) — seed task set

tasks.question: Who is the account lead on the Definite engagement?
signals: names Trevor Fox as the lead on Definite explicitly
references the /clients/definite node or its `led-by` edge as the source
grounds the claim in graph content rather than guessing from the founder's role
tasks.notes: Discovery via simplest path: from root, find /clients/definite, surface the led-by edge pointing at /core/people/trevor. Tests the load-bearing weak_edge_description.missing case — the led-by edge has no description, so the agent must rely on the type-plus-target pattern.

tasks.question: Which active EA clients receive SEO + Content service, and what's the scope of each engagement?
signals: lists at least the active clients that receive SEO + Content (Definite, Looker Pros at minimum)
describes each client's engagement scope using language drawn from the graph (e.g. 'SEO + Content with outbound execution via Segun' for Definite)
uses the /core/services/seo-content node's `applied-to` edges or the client-side `served-by` edges as the basis for the answer
does not invent clients that aren't in the graph
tasks.notes: Multi-node synthesis: requires reading /core/services/seo-content to find applied-to clients, then drilling into at least 2-3 of those clients to surface scope. Tests whether the typed-edge vocabulary (applied-to, served-by) is informative enough on its own.

tasks.question: What SEO patterns has EA formalized, and where have they been deployed in client work?
signals: names the SEO Analysis Routine as a formalized pattern
mentions the service-page-workflow-template as the other pattern under /patterns/seo
identifies that the analysis routine is deployed at /clients/definite/seo (or names Definite as the first deployment)
correctly reports that the patterns are still in draft status if drilling into them
tasks.notes: Pattern lookup with applies-pattern backlinks: from /patterns/seo, find the patterns; for each, follow incoming references to find where deployed. Tests whether the patterns subtree's deliberate use of cross-references (applies-pattern incoming edges) is traversable.

tasks.question: What three internal projects is EA building, and what makes Spandrel different from Context Cannon?
signals: lists all three projects (Spandrel, Context Cannon, Flux)
explains Spandrel as open-source markdown-to-graph framework
explains Context Cannon as the hosted/SaaS version of Spandrel
captures the relationship that Cannon is essentially hosted Spandrel — not a separate idea
doesn't conflate the two products
tasks.notes: Cross-collection synthesis + comparison. Spandrel and Cannon share DNA; the distinction is in deployment model. Tests whether the project-node descriptions are differentiated enough to surface the comparison without reading both bodies.

tasks.question: Has EA ever worked with a healthcare or medical client based in Europe?
signals: states clearly that no European healthcare client appears in the graph
may note Inner Balance as a US medical clinic (the closest match) but distinguishes it as US-based, not European
does not fabricate clients or geographies
phrases the negative answer confidently rather than hedging into a wrong answer
tasks.notes: Negative-answer test: the right answer is 'no, not in the graph.' Inner Balance is the closest match (US medical clinic, YMYL SEO) and could trip an agent into a false positive if it doesn't read the descriptions carefully. Critical that the agent doesn't hallucinate.

tasks.question: What is EA's locked Q2 2026 direction, and what is each team member responsible for?
signals: names the three OKRs (Flux to $300 MRR, Spandrel to 100 stars + 5 active users, Cannon for internal trust + first paid external)
identifies Trevor as owning positioning/strategy across all three
identifies Robert's responsibilities (Flux onboarding/support, Spandrel external users, Cannon migrations)
identifies Segun's responsibilities (Flux outbound ops, Cannon client setup, Spandrel internal usage)
references the /core/direction/q2-2026 node as the source
tasks.notes: Recency-sensitive + multi-attribution: the q2-2026 doc is the canonical artifact. Body content is dense (specific KRs per project, owners per KR). Tests whether deep body content gets surfaced rather than only frontmatter.

tasks.question: What tools and runbooks make up EA's day-to-day operations infrastructure?
signals: lists at least four of: ClickUp, EA-OS Gateway, Google Workspace groups, OS overview, Setup MCPs runbook, Skills index
names ClickUp as the project management system
mentions the MCP setup runbook for installing agent integrations
uses /core/ops as the source
does not over-fetch into client subgraphs that aren't relevant to ops
tasks.notes: Hierarchy traversal into a focused subtree. /core/ops has 6 children, all leaves with informative descriptions. Tests whether the agent can stop at the right level — no need to drill into each leaf's body.

tasks.question: What specifically does Segun do on the Definite engagement?
signals: describes Segun's role as outbound execution (Apollo sequences, n8n automations, or cold campaigns)
grounds the answer in Segun's profile (/core/people/segun) and/or the works-with edge from /clients/definite
doesn't over-state Segun's scope on Definite (he's outbound-focused, not full-account)
tasks.notes: Edge-description reliance test. The works-with edge from /clients/definite to /core/people/segun has no description; the agent has to read both nodes' descriptions and bodies to triangulate. Tests the load-bearing case where edge descriptions are missing but the surrounding context recovers.

tasks.question: Both Definite and Looker Pros are EA clients in the analytics space — what distinguishes their engagement scopes?
signals: identifies Definite's scope as SEO + Content with outbound execution
identifies Looker Pros' scope as Web Dev, SEO, and SEM (broader than Definite's)
captures that Definite is the strategic/active engagement vs Looker Pros being a different shape entirely
doesn't conflate the two engagements
may note that both touch SEO but with different surrounding service mixes
tasks.notes: Comparison / conflict-resolution. Both clients are 'analytics-adjacent' but the engagement scopes differ. Tests whether the agent can hold two client nodes side-by-side and surface the meaningful difference, not just the surface similarity.

tasks.question: What are the two positioning collapses Definite's team actively works to avoid, and why does each matter?
signals: names 'It's a BI tool' as one collapse
names 'It's a cheaper modern data stack' as the other collapse
explains why the BI-tool collapse matters (mentally categorized as dashboarding, assumes underlying data system already works)
explains why the cheaper-stack collapse matters (reduces conversation to component-by-component cost comparison)
uses the /clients/definite/collapses subtree as the source
tasks.notes: Deep client-subgraph traversal. Definite's collapses are explicit nodes under /clients/definite/collapses with substantive descriptions. Tests whether the agent navigates into client positioning subgraphs and reads the structured positioning content rather than the parent node's body.
